content.text: Why a Structured Comms Plan — Not Just Good Communication

GenAI adoption programmes that rely on ad hoc communication consistently underperform programmes that operate from a structured plan. The failure mode is not that people are not told — it is that the right people are not told the right things at the right time in the right sequence. An executive who has not been briefed on how to respond to 'Will AI replace jobs?' will improvise an answer that contradicts the official narrative. A manager who receives the same all-hands message as their team cannot serve as a credible explainer. A plan makes the difference between a communications programme and a communications campaign.

A leader and employee communications plan is not a schedule of emails. It is a structured engagement programme that treats different roles in the organisation as different audiences with different information needs, credibility requirements, and influence over adoption success.

attrs.alt: GenAI Leader–Employee Comms Cascade — Executive, BU Leader, Manager, and Employee tiers

content.text: The cascade model distributes communications responsibility across three tiers. Each tier translates the message from the tier above into the context of the people below, which means the message becomes progressively more specific and role-relevant as it travels down. The critical discipline is that briefing at each tier happens before, not simultaneously with, the tier below — a manager cannot credibly brief their team on a change they learned about in the same all-hands meeting as everyone else.

Executive leaders own the strategic narrative: why the organisation is adopting GenAI now, what it means for the organisation's competitive position, and what the long-term vision is. They do not own the role-specific operational detail — that is for the tiers below. Their credibility comes from positioning the change in the context of where the organisation is going, not from demonstrating technical depth.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum


class AudienceScope(Enum):
    WHOLE_ORGANISATION = "whole_org"
    BUSINESS_UNIT = "business_unit"
    FUNCTION = "function"
    TEAM = "team"
    INDIVIDUAL = "individual"


@dataclass
class CommsEvent:
    title: str
    owner: str
    audience_scope: AudienceScope
    scheduled_date: date
    channel: str
    duration_minutes: int
    key_messages: list[str]
    preparation_required: list[str]
    follow_on_actions: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def briefing_due_date(self, days_before: int = 7) -> date:
        from datetime import timedelta
        return date(
            self.scheduled_date.year,
            self.scheduled_date.month,
            self.scheduled_date.day
        ) - timedelta(days=days_before)

    def is_ready(self) -> bool:
        return bool(self.key_messages) and bool(self.preparation_required)


# Executive launch event
exec_launch = CommsEvent(
    title="GenAI Programme Launch — Executive Briefing",
    owner="CEO",
    audience_scope=AudienceScope.WHOLE_ORGANISATION,
    scheduled_date=date(2026, 9, 1),
    channel="All-hands (live + recorded)",
    duration_minutes=30,
    key_messages=[
        "Why we are adopting GenAI now — capability gap and competitive context",
        "The scope of the initial programme — which functions, what tools",
        "What 'success' looks like in 12 months (specific, not abstract)",
        "How decisions about AI use are made and who is accountable",
        "Honest acknowledgement that this involves change and that change is hard",
    ],
    preparation_required=[
        "CHRO brief on expected employee concerns by function",
        "Legal brief on what can and cannot be said about headcount",
        "Pilot data summary — results and limitations, not only successes",
        "Manager FAQ document finalised before this event",
    ],
    follow_on_actions=[
        "Recording and transcript published within 24 hours",
        "Q&A themes summarised and distributed to managers by end of week",
    ],
)

print(f"Event: {exec_launch.title}")
print(f"Owner: {exec_launch.owner}")
print(f"Briefing due: {exec_launch.briefing_due_date()}")
print(f"Ready: {exec_launch.is_ready()}")
What Executive Leaders Should Not Say

Certain phrasings consistently damage trust when used by senior leaders. They signal either that leadership has not thought through the implications, or that it is deliberately managing perception rather than sharing reality.

— This is almost never true for all roles at once. Some roles gain capability; some roles change significantly. The statement is heard as either naive or dishonest by the employees whose roles change most.
'No jobs will be lost because of this.'

— Unless this is specifically scoped (e.g., 'in this programme phase') and legally reviewable, saying it forecloses honest future communication about workforce impact. If jobs might be affected by a different programme, this statement will be recalled.
— 'Productivity gains' is heard by employees as headcount reduction in a different frame. Use specific capability or quality improvements instead.
'Other companies are doing this, so we have to as well.'
— Peer pressure framing reduces psychological safety and increases anxiety. Explain the specific benefit your organisation is trying to capture.

Business unit leaders and senior managers translate the executive narrative into the context of their function. A legal team leader explains the narrative differently to their team than an engineering director does. This tier requires pre-briefing and must happen before BU-level communication is sent to the broader team.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class BULeaderBriefingKit:
    bu_name: str
    impacted_roles: list[str]
    pilot_results_for_bu: Optional[str]
    top_concerns_anticipated: list[str]
    approved_talking_points: list[str]
    questions_to_escalate: list[str]    # do NOT answer in-BU; route to OCM/HR
    faq_document_url: str

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        issues = []
        if not self.pilot_results_for_bu:
            issues.append(
                "No BU-specific pilot data — leader must not claim benefits without evidence"
            )
        if not self.approved_talking_points:
            issues.append("No approved talking points — leader will improvise")
        if not self.questions_to_escalate:
            issues.append(
                "No escalation list defined — leader may answer questions outside their authority"
            )
        return issues


legal_bu_kit = BULeaderBriefingKit(
    bu_name="Legal Services",
    impacted_roles=["Legal Analyst", "Contracts Manager", "Paralegal"],
    pilot_results_for_bu=(
        "8-week pilot with 12 legal analysts: avg clause extraction time reduced from "
        "67 min to 14 min review. 3 hallucination incidents detected and caught by "
        "analysts during review. No client-facing errors."
    ),
    top_concerns_anticipated=[
        "Will AI errors expose us to professional liability?",
        "Will junior analyst roles be reduced?",
        "How does the firm's professional indemnity insurance treat AI-assisted work?",
    ],
    approved_talking_points=[
        "The analyst reviews and signs off every AI output — legal accountability does not change",
        "We are starting with clause extraction, not advice or drafting",
        "Pilot showed the tool has specific failure modes — we are training to them",
    ],
    questions_to_escalate=[
        "Will headcount be reduced in the next 12 months?",
        "Is this connected to the restructuring programme?",
        "What happens if I refuse to use the tool?",
    ],
    faq_document_url="/internal/genai/legal-faq-v3",
)

issues = legal_bu_kit.validate()
if issues:
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"  ⚠ {issue}")
else:
    print(f"BU briefing kit for '{legal_bu_kit.bu_name}' validated — ready to distribute")
    print(f"Anticipated concerns: {len(legal_bu_kit.top_concerns_anticipated)}")
    print(f"Questions to escalate (do not answer locally): {len(legal_bu_kit.questions_to_escalate)}")

Line managers are the most important channel in any major organisational change — and the most under-prepared. They are the people employees will actually ask their questions to, and if they cannot answer credibly, they lose trust and the programme loses credibility by association. Manager enablement is not a briefing session; it is a structured preparation programme.
Each manager who will be briefing their team on the GenAI adoption should complete the following before any team-level communication:
Attend the manager briefing session (not the employee all-hands — a separate, earlier session)
Read the FAQ document and identify which questions they cannot answer (to route to OCM)
Review the role-specific impact summary for their team's roles
Practice answering the top 5 anticipated questions using approved talking points
Know the escalation path: where to direct employees for questions outside their authority
Understand the feedback channel and how to promote it to their team

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


class ManagerReadinessStatus(Enum):
    NOT_STARTED = "not_started"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    READY = "ready"
    NEEDS_SUPPORT = "needs_support"


@dataclass
class ManagerReadinessRecord:
    manager_name: str
    team_size: int
    impacted_roles_in_team: list[str]
    briefing_attended: bool
    faq_reviewed: bool
    role_impact_reviewed: bool
    practice_session_completed: bool
    escalation_path_confirmed: bool
    feedback_channel_understood: bool

    @property
    def readiness_score(self) -> int:
        checks = [
            self.briefing_attended,
            self.faq_reviewed,
            self.role_impact_reviewed,
            self.practice_session_completed,
            self.escalation_path_confirmed,
            self.feedback_channel_understood,
        ]
        return sum(1 for c in checks if c)

    @property
    def status(self) -> ManagerReadinessStatus:
        score = self.readiness_score
        if score == 6:
            return ManagerReadinessStatus.READY
        elif score >= 4:
            return ManagerReadinessStatus.IN_PROGRESS
        elif score >= 2:
            return ManagerReadinessStatus.NEEDS_SUPPORT
        return ManagerReadinessStatus.NOT_STARTED

    def gaps(self) -> list[str]:
        result = []
        if not self.briefing_attended:
            result.append("Has not attended manager briefing session")
        if not self.faq_reviewed:
            result.append("FAQ document not reviewed")
        if not self.role_impact_reviewed:
            result.append("Role-specific impact not reviewed")
        if not self.practice_session_completed:
            result.append("Q&A practice session not completed")
        if not self.escalation_path_confirmed:
            result.append("Escalation path not confirmed")
        if not self.feedback_channel_understood:
            result.append("Feedback channel not understood")
        return result


managers = [
    ManagerReadinessRecord(
        manager_name="J. Hartley",
        team_size=8,
        impacted_roles_in_team=["Legal Analyst", "Paralegal"],
        briefing_attended=True,
        faq_reviewed=True,
        role_impact_reviewed=True,
        practice_session_completed=False,
        escalation_path_confirmed=True,
        feedback_channel_understood=True,
    ),
    ManagerReadinessRecord(
        manager_name="P. Singh",
        team_size=12,
        impacted_roles_in_team=["Contracts Manager"],
        briefing_attended=False,
        faq_reviewed=False,
        role_impact_reviewed=False,
        practice_session_completed=False,
        escalation_path_confirmed=False,
        feedback_channel_understood=False,
    ),
]

for mgr in managers:
    print(f"{mgr.manager_name} — {mgr.status.value} ({mgr.readiness_score}/6)")
    for gap in mgr.gaps():
        print(f"  → {gap}")

The employee-facing communications track consists of a sequence of touchpoints, not a single announcement. Each touchpoint has a specific purpose, owner, and format. The sequencing matters: employees need to understand the strategic context before receiving role-specific detail, and role-specific detail before receiving operational instruction.
Brief managers before employees — separate event
Strategic context: why now, what is in scope, what is not
Role-specific impact: what changes in your day, what stays same
Support and readiness: training schedule, escalation path, feedback channel
Final confirmation: go-live date, first-day steps, who to contact
Manager has verbally confirmed with each team member
Feedback loop close: here is what we heard, here is what changed

Not all communications channels are equivalent for all message types. High-anxiety content (job impact, accountability changes) should not arrive in an email with no opportunity for response. Low-complexity operational updates (training reminder, FAQ link) do not require a live event. Matching channel to message type prevents both under-communication of critical topics and over-communication fatigue.
All-hands (live preferred), CEO video, intranet summary
All-hands email blast; Slack channel post
Legal-reviewed written comms + live Q&A session
Named-owner written statement + manager verbal follow-up
Informal Slack message; rumour response only
Written update referencing specific changes made

A communications plan without a structured feedback loop is one-directional and will not build the trust required for sustained adoption. The feedback loop has three components: a collection mechanism, an analysis function, and a closed-loop response that shows employees their input was heard.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class ConcernCategory(Enum):
    JOB_SECURITY = "job_security"
    ACCOUNTABILITY = "accountability"
    QUALITY_TRUST = "quality_trust"
    TRAINING_READINESS = "training_readiness"
    TOOL_RELIABILITY = "tool_reliability"
    PROCESS_UNCLEAR = "process_unclear"
    OTHER = "other"


@dataclass
class EmployeeConcern:
    submission_id: str
    role: str
    category: ConcernCategory
    concern_text: str
    submitted_anonymously: bool
    resolved: bool = False
    response_published: Optional[str] = None

    def requires_escalation(self) -> bool:
        high_sensitivity = {
            ConcernCategory.JOB_SECURITY,
            ConcernCategory.ACCOUNTABILITY,
        }
        return self.category in high_sensitivity


@dataclass
class FeedbackLoopReport:
    period: str
    total_submissions: int
    concerns: list[EmployeeConcern]

    def by_category(self) -> dict[ConcernCategory, int]:
        counts: dict[ConcernCategory, int] = {}
        for concern in self.concerns:
            counts[concern.category] = counts.get(concern.category, 0) + 1
        return counts

    def resolution_rate(self) -> float:
        if not self.concerns:
            return 0.0
        return sum(1 for c in self.concerns if c.resolved) / len(self.concerns)

    def unresolved_escalations(self) -> list[EmployeeConcern]:
        return [
            c for c in self.concerns
            if c.requires_escalation() and not c.resolved
        ]

    def generate_close_loop_summary(self) -> str:
        by_cat = self.by_category()
        top_category = max(by_cat, key=by_cat.get) if by_cat else None
        unresolved = self.unresolved_escalations()
        return (
            f"Feedback period: {self.period}\n"
            f"Total submissions: {self.total_submissions}\n"
            f"Top concern category: {top_category.value if top_category else 'none'}\n"
            f"Resolution rate: {self.resolution_rate():.0%}\n"
            f"Unresolved escalations: {len(unresolved)}"
        )


# Example: Wave 1 post-comms feedback
concerns = [
    EmployeeConcern("FB-001", "Legal Analyst", ConcernCategory.ACCOUNTABILITY,
        "Who is responsible when AI output is wrong and a client is harmed?",
        True, resolved=True,
        response_published="The lawyer who reviews and signs off the AI output carries full professional accountability. "
                           "This is documented in the updated engagement letter clause — see FAQ Q12."),
    EmployeeConcern("FB-002", "Paralegal", ConcernCategory.JOB_SECURITY,
        "Is this connected to the headcount review mentioned in Q2?",
        True, resolved=False),
    EmployeeConcern("FB-003", "Contracts Manager", ConcernCategory.TOOL_RELIABILITY,
        "What happens if the tool is down and I have a contract deadline?",
        False, resolved=True,
        response_published="Manual extraction process documented in Confluence — link in FAQ Q7."),
]

report = FeedbackLoopReport(
    period="Wave 1 (week 1–2 post-comms)",
    total_submissions=3,
    concerns=concerns,
)

print(report.generate_close_loop_summary())
print()
unresolved = report.unresolved_escalations()
if unresolved:
    print("Unresolved escalations requiring HR / senior leader response:")
    for c in unresolved:
        print(f"  {c.submission_id}: {c.concern_text[:80]}...")
Connecting the Communications Plan to Adoption Metrics

The communications plan is not an end in itself — it is an input to adoption. The plan should be measured by whether it produces the adoption behaviour it is designed to enable, not by whether all planned events took place. An organisation that ran all five communication waves but saw no improvement in comprehension or trust has run a comms programme, not achieved a communications outcome.
— test whether employees can describe what will change in their role after Wave 2. Employees who cannot articulate the change are unlikely to engage with training meaningfully.
— a leading indicator of voluntary adoption. Employees who complete training on time are significantly more likely to adopt the tool within the first month.
— tracks whether employees who completed training are actually using the tool. Low activation after training indicates a barrier at the workflow level, not the awareness level.

— a leading indicator of psychological safety. A channel that receives no submissions has either no engaged audience or a perceived risk to submitting. Low usage should be investigated before go-live.

— managers who cannot answer their team's top questions are a bottleneck. A pre-launch manager confidence survey (one question: 'I can answer my team's top 3 questions') surfaces this before it becomes a trust issue.
Manager briefing happens same day as all-hands
Planning treated comms as simultaneous, not sequenced
Insert a mandatory gap (≥5 working days) between manager briefing and employee all-hands in the plan template
BU leader delivers a different message than the CEO
No shared talking-point alignment before events
Mandatory talking-point sign-off with all BU leaders 72h before the all-hands
Employees perceive risk in submitting; channel is not promoted
Manager verbally promotes channel in team briefing; first 10 submissions get a named response from OCM lead
Programme moved on; OCM team reallocated
Wave 5 is planned and calendared before Wave 1 occurs; has a named owner separate from the programme delivery team
Legal function receives no role-specific messaging
Role impact matrix forces explicit confirmation that each impacted role has a Wave 2 comms event assigned to it

A leader and employee communications plan structures GenAI adoption communications across a three-tier cascade: executive leaders set the strategic narrative, business unit leaders translate it into functional context, and line managers deliver role-specific impact. The plan must brief each tier before the tier below — a manager cannot brief their team on something they learned about in the same all-hands meeting. Employee communications are sequenced across five waves from six weeks pre-launch to four weeks post-launch, with each wave serving a distinct purpose. Channel selection matches message sensitivity to format: high-anxiety topics (job impact, accountability) require live conversational settings, not broadcast email. The feedback loop closes the loop: employees must see evidence that their input changed something before they trust the programme. Adoption metrics — comprehension rate, tool activation, manager confidence — connect the communications plan to programme outcomes and show whether the investment in structured communications is producing the adoption behaviour the organisation needs.
